commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=793c60c01983ea06e3428722d98728c9d6f7dbef)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds explicit failover classification for `upstream_error` and `server_error`, relaxes provider error-type extraction to accept lowercase snake case, and adds focused failover-error tests.

PR surface: Source +41, Tests +60. Total +101 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level: current main wires embedded run results through `classifyEmbeddedAgentRunResultForModelFallback`, and that classifier returns null for non-business transient provider payloads before the fallback loop can advance. I did not run a live provider repro in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] The PR needs human review and contributor proof because the code repair is incomplete and the real-behavior proof gate is unmet.

target.title: fix(agents): classify upstream_error as fallbackable to harden model fallback
target.body:
## fix(agents): classify upstream_error as fallbackable to harden model fallback

## Summary

When an OpenAI-compatible provider returns `errorType: "upstream_error"`, OpenClaw's failover classification pipeline does not recognise it as a fallbackable error. The request terminates immediately instead of trying the next configured fallback model. This PR hardens the classification pipeline so that `upstream_error` — a standard OpenAI API error type indicating a transient upstream backend failure — is correctly classified as fallbackable, enabling model fallback to recover from it automatically.

This is a defensive hardening change: the core model-fallback loop already continues past unrecognised errors when remaining candidates exist, but without proper classification the error is opaque — no structured `FailoverError`, no `reason`, no `emitFailoverEvent`, no remediation hint. Classifying `upstream_error` explicitly ensures the fallback loop has the full signal it needs to make optimal decisions (cooldown, skip-cache, suspension) and that users see actionable error messages instead of generic failures.

The real scenario (openclaw/openclaw#95519): a provider returns

```json
{"error":{"message":"Upstream request failed","type":"upstream_error","param":"","code":null}}
```

Before this fix, OpenClaw ends the turn with a generic failure. After this fix, `upstream_error` is classified as `timeout` (fallbackable) and the fallback loop tries the next model.

## Why this matters

`upstream_error` is a standard OpenAI API error type — it means the provider's upstream backend failed. It is returned by any OpenAI-compatible provider (OpenAI itself, Azure OpenAI, or third-party OpenAI-compatible gateways like LiteLLM, OneAPI) when their upstream model service has a transient outage. This is not a rare edge case; it occurs whenever an OpenAI-compatible provider's backend is temporarily unavailable.

**Before this fix**, every such outage results in a degraded user experience — no automatic recovery, no structured error, no diagnostic signal — even though model fallback is designed to handle exactly this class of transient failure.

**After this fix**, `upstream_error` is correctly classified as a transient, fallbackable error (`timeout`). When fallback models are configured, the system automatically retries on an alternate model and the user gets a normal response after a brief delay.

## Real user impact

**When `upstream_error` is correctly classified as fallbackable (after this fix):**
- The model-fallback loop recognises the error as a known `FailoverError` with reason `timeout`.
- If fallback models are configured, the loop skips the failed candidate and tries the next one — the user gets a response from the fallback model, possibly with a brief delay.
- If all candidates fail, a structured `FallbackSummaryError` is thrown with per-attempt details and a remediation hint (e.g. "All models failed (2): openai/gpt-5.4: Upstream request failed (timeout) | anthropic/claude: ...").
- The `emitFailoverEvent` diagnostic fires, enabling observability into which provider/model failed and why.
- The cooldown and skip-cache logic receive a structured `reason`, allowing optimal decisions on subsequent turns.

**When `upstream_error` is NOT classified as fallbackable (before this fix):**
- `coerceToFailoverError` returns `null` because `resolveFailoverReasonFromError` cannot determine a reason.
- In the model-fallback loop, `normalized` falls back to the raw `err` object. `isFailoverError(normalized)` is `false`.
- If this is the **only candidate** (no fallback configured), or the **last candidate** in the loop, the raw error is thrown directly — **not** wrapped in a `FallbackSummaryError`. No structured attempts, no remediation hint, no `emitFailoverEvent`.
- The user sees the generic failure message: `"⚠️ Something went wrong while processing your request. Please try again, or use /new to start a fresh session."` instead of a specific, actionable error.
- Even when other candidates remain and the loop continues, the unclassified error is recorded without a `reason` in the attempts array, losing diagnostic signal that would otherwise help the cooldown and skip-cache logic optimise subsequent turns.

In short: **a transient, recoverable error is treated as an opaque, unclassified failure.** The fallback loop may still continue, but without the structured signal it needs for optimal decision-making, and the user sees a generic error message instead of a specific, actionable one.

## Root Cause

Two classification gaps directly cause the #95519 failure:

1. **`isStableProviderErrorType` rejects lowercase snake_case.** The original regex `/^[A-Z][A-Z0-9_:-]*$/` only matches `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE` (e.g. `PROVIDER_QUOTA_EXCEEDED`). Since `upstream_error` is lowercase, `readDirectErrorType` returns `undefined` → `signal.errorType` is `undefined`.

2. **No classifier recognises `"upstream_error"` as a transient error type.** Even if `errorType` were successfully extracted, nothing mapped `"upstream_error"` to a fallbackable reason. The existing `classifyFailoverReasonFromCode` has no match for `code: null`, and `classifyFailoverReasonFromErrorType` did not exist yet.

A third, secondary gap exists but does **not** affect the #95519 scenario directly:

3. **`API_ERROR_TRANSIENT_SIGNALS_RE` only matches `"upstream error"` (space), not `"upstream_error"` (underscore).** This regex is used by `isJsonApiInternalServerError`, which scans the raw message text as a fallback classification path. It activates when the outer error type is `api_error` — a type explicitly excluded by `isStableProviderErrorType` as too generic — so `signal.errorType` is `undefined` and classification falls through to message-based matching. In the #95519 payload, the outer wrapper `type` is `"error"` (not `"api_error"`), and `findErrorProperty` recurses into the nested `error` object whose `type` is `"upstream_error"` (not `"api_error"` either). So this regex path is **not** reached for the #95519 case; it serves as defense-in-depth for a hypothetical payload shape like `{"type":"error","error":{"type":"api_error","message":"upstream_error: ..."}}` where the inner error type is `api_error` and only the message text contains the transient signal.

## Fix

Two primary changes fix #95519:

1. **New `classifyFailoverReasonFromErrorType` function** (`errors.ts`): Classifies known transient provider error types from the `errorType` field directly. `upstream_error` → `timeout`, `server_error` → `server_error` — both are fallbackable. Unknown types return `null` and fall through to message-based classification. This is placed early in `classifyFailoverSignal`, before message-based classification, so structured error types are always prioritised.

2. **Extended `isStableProviderErrorType` regex** (`failover-error.ts`): Now accepts `lowercase_snake_case` in addition to `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE`, so `readDirectErrorType` can extract error types like `upstream_error` from nested payloads.

One secondary defense-in-depth change:

3. **Changed `upstream error` → `upstream.error` in `API_ERROR_TRANSIENT_SIGNALS_RE`** (`errors.ts`): This makes the regex match both `"upstream error"` (space) and `"upstream_error"` (underscore) when scanning message text via `isJsonApiInternalServerError`. It does not affect the #95519 scenario directly, but ensures that if a provider ever returns an `api_error`-typed payload whose message contains `upstream_error`, it will be correctly classified as transient.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Provider returns `errorType: "upstream_error"` → OpenClaw now classifies it as a fallbackable `timeout` instead of leaving it unclassified, which hardens the model-fallback loop with structured signal and gives users actionable error messages instead of generic failures.

**Environment tested:** Local checkout on branch `fix/fallback-upstream-error-underscore`, Node 22.x on Linux.

**Steps run after the patch:**

```bash
npx vitest run src/agents/failover-error.test.ts
```

**Evidence — new test cases pass:**

```
✓ |unit-fast| src/agents/failover-error.test.ts > upstream_error type triggers fallback > classifies upstream_error errorType as timeout (fallbackable)
✓ |unit-fast| src/agents/failover-error.test.ts > upstream_error type triggers fallback > classifies server_error errorType as server_error (fallbackable)
✓ |unit-fast| src/agents/failover-error.test.ts > upstream_error type triggers fallback > resolves upstream_error via resolveFailoverReasonFromError with nested payload
✓ |unit-fast| src/agents/failover-error.test.ts > upstream_error type triggers fallback > does not classify unknown errorType as fallbackable
✓ |unit-fast| src/agents/failover-error.test.ts > upstream_error type triggers fallback > classifies upstream_error via JSON api_error transient signals (regex)
```

**Evidence — full suite result:**

```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  96 passed (96)
   Duration  1.00s (transform 344ms, setup 0ms, import 696ms, tests 76ms, environment 0ms)
```
target.author: hugenshen

commands.reason: repair commands require a ClawSweeper PR or a PR opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
